skill.name: agent-coordination
skill.description: Coordinate autonomous coding agents through VibeKanban workflows, task templates, delegation patterns, and CI monitoring guidance.
skill.summary: Coordinate coding agents with structured VibeKanban task delegation.
skill.author: ClementWalter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, credential, filesystem, and network indicators, but most are markdown examples or API documentation rather than executable behavior. The confirmed concerns are legitimate for this skill type: it can direct coding agents through VibeKanban, includes a local autonomy exception, and ships a CI helper that invokes the GitHub CLI and reads CI logs.

medium_findings.title: Autonomous Agent Dispatch Can Modify Repositories
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to create VibeKanban tasks and start workspace sessions with coding executors. This matches the skill purpose, but it can delegate code changes to autonomous agents and should be used only with trusted projects and review gates.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly document starting workspace sessions with coding executors. This is intended functionality, but it expands operational impact beyond passive guidance.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Role Override and Local Autonomy Exception
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the assistant to persist Chief of Staff mode for the conversation and grants full autonomy in a hardcoded local marketplace path. This is not clearly malicious, but it changes normal execution boundaries and may surprise users outside the original author environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicit in the skill instructions. It does not request bypassing security review, but it does alter role persistence and execution permissions.

medium_findings.title: GitHub CLI Subprocess and CI Log Exposure
medium_findings.description: The bundled CI monitor runs the GitHub CLI and prints failed job logs. Arguments are passed as a list without shell expansion, reducing command injection risk, but the helper still performs authenticated network operations and may display sensitive CI output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess use is real, but it calls a fixed executable with structured arguments and no shell. The remaining concern is exposure through GitHub access and printed logs.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command findings point to fenced markdown, inline examples, and MCP call documentation. These lines are instructional text and do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations are markdown templates or tool-call examples. No evidence found that these snippets execute automatically when the skill is loaded.

low_findings.title: Environment and Filesystem References Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The environment and filesystem findings are example task descriptions about renaming branding variables and env files. No evidence found that the skill reads secrets or directly opens environment files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited references are markdown examples and placeholder task text. They mention env files and formatted descriptions, but do not implement file access.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub URLs Are Limited to Documentation and CLI Parsing
low_findings.description: The network indicators are GitHub example URLs, an install URL for the GitHub CLI, and regex parsing of GitHub pull request URLs. No evidence found of data exfiltration to an untrusted endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All reviewed URLs target GitHub or GitHub CLI documentation. The script uses GitHub CLI operations for PR status monitoring, consistent with its stated purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The CI monitor invokes `gh` through subprocess.run. The call avoids shell expansion, but still depends on local credentials and external GitHub access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess pattern is confirmed and security impact is limited by fixed executable invocation and argument-list usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent Workspace Session Launch
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow starts workspace sessions with coding executors. This is core functionality, but it can cause code changes through delegated agents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly launch agent sessions. The risk depends on user-selected projects, permissions, and executor configuration.

content.value_statement: Managing several coding agents can create unclear ownership, weak task briefs, and missed follow-up. This skill provides VibeKanban workflows, templates, and monitoring guidance for structured delegation.
actual_capabilities: Defines a Chief of Staff coordination mode for coding agent teams.
Provides VibeKanban task creation and workspace session workflows.
Includes templates for bugs, features, chores, investigations, and migrations.
Explains two-tier delegation using investigation and implementation tasks.
Provides a Python helper for GitHub pull request CI monitoring.
Documents status reporting, escalation, and backlog display patterns.
limitations: Requires VibeKanban MCP tools or equivalent workflow support.
Does not implement project management storage by itself.
The CI helper requires the GitHub CLI and repository access.
Delegated agents still need human review for risky code changes.

use_cases.title: Coordinate a Feature Build
use_cases.description: Break a feature into investigation and implementation tasks, assign agents, and track progress through review.

use_cases.title: Triage Multiple Bugs
use_cases.description: Create clear bug tasks with reproduction steps, scope boundaries, and acceptance criteria for parallel agent work.

use_cases.title: Monitor Pull Request CI
use_cases.description: Check pull request status and surface failed CI logs while agents continue implementation work.

prompt_templates.title: Start Coordination
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin a Chief of Staff session for one project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Act as my Chief of Staff for this repository. List the available VibeKanban projects, help me choose the right one, and show the current backlog.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Bug Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn a bug report into an agent-ready task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a VibeKanban task for this bug: [describe bug]. Include context, reproduction steps, acceptance criteria, and out-of-scope items.

prompt_templates.title: Split Investigation and Implementation
prompt_templates.scenario: Use two-tier delegation for unclear work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the two-tier delegation pattern for this goal: [goal]. Create an investigation task first, then define the implementation task that should follow.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Parallel Delivery
prompt_templates.scenario: Manage several coding agents across active tasks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the active VibeKanban tasks, identify blockers, assign available agents, and provide a concise status table with recommended next actions.

output_examples.input: Create a task to fix the mobile login button.
output_examples.output: A bug task with problem statement, Safari context, reproduction steps, acceptance criteria, and an agent assignment plan.

output_examples.input: Show me the project backlog and what is blocked.
output: A status table grouped by task state.
Recommended follow-up decisions for the user.

output_examples.output: A concise CI summary showing passed, pending, or failed checks, with failure details when available.

best_practices: Use clear acceptance criteria before assigning an autonomous agent.
Keep investigation tasks separate from implementation tasks when scope is uncertain.
Review agent output and CI results before merging repository changes.
anti_patterns: Creating vague tasks that omit context, scope, or verification steps.
Letting agents modify sensitive files without explicit review boundaries.
Treating CI monitoring output as a substitute for human code review.

faq.question: What does this skill coordinate?
faq.answer: It coordinates coding agents through VibeKanban tasks, workspace sessions, status reports, and escalation workflows.

faq.question: Does it write code directly?
faq.answer: The skill is designed for coordination. It delegates implementation to agents and monitors the resulting work.

faq.question: Which tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects VibeKanban MCP tools for task management and the GitHub CLI for the optional CI monitor.

faq.question: Can it manage several agents at once?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides patterns for parallel task assignment, backlog tracking, and blocker escalation.

faq.question: Is this safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use it only with trusted agents and repositories. Review task scope, logs, and code changes before merge.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill intentionally starts coding agent sessions and includes a GitHub CLI script. These are useful but require permission controls.
